title-group.article-title: Right parasternal echocardiographic reference values in the healthy male Iranian Shall sheep

aff.named-content: Department of Animal and Poultry Health and Nutrition

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceSirous Sadeghian Chaleshtori and Mohammad Reza Mokber Dezfouli, Department of Internal Medicine, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Institute of Biomedical Research, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran. Email: s.sadeghian@ut.ac.ir and mokhberd@ut.ac.ir

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Among the large animals, the heart of sheep is functionally and structurally very similar to the human heart. In research, sheep are used as an animal model to study the process of cardiac disease pathogenesis and treatment. Therefore, determining the normal values of the heart structures of sheep with echocardiography is of particular emphasis. The purpose of the present research is to define the normal echocardiography values of heart in Iranian Shall breed sheep.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: In 20 healthy Iranian Shall male sheep weighing 30–35 kg and aged 4–6 months, standing echocardiography was done from the right parasternal approach concentrated on the 3rd to 5th intercostal spaces by 2.5–5 MHz phased array transducer in the longitudinal and transverse views by B‐mode, M‐mode and Doppler systems. In M‐mode echocardiographic, the parameters of interventricular septal, left ventricular internal diameter, left ventricular free wall, right ventricular free wall and right ventricular internal diameter in diastole and systole as well as end point septal separation, ejection fraction (EF) slope, aortic root diameter, left atrial appendage, left atrial diameter/aortic valve diameter, left ventricular ejection time, fractional shortening, end‐diastolic volume, end‐systolic volume, EF, stroke volume and cardiac output and in pulsed‐wave spectral Doppler echocardiographic, the parameters of mitral valve maximum velocity (V max), mitral valve mean velocity (V mean), mitral valve maximum pressure gradient (PGmax), mitral valve mean pressure gradient (PGmean), mitral valve velocity time integral (VTI), mitral valve E‐wave (MV‐E), MV‐E pressure gradient, mitral valve A‐wave (MV‐A), MV‐A pressure gradient, aortic V max, aortic valve V mean, aortic valve PGmax, aortic valve PGmean, aortic valve VTI, left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT)‐V max, LVOT‐V mean were measured.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: All the sheep in this study were healthy and had no signs of heart disease. In this study, the parameters of M‐mode and spectral Doppler echocardiographic were assessed and recognized in Iranian Shall sheep. The results demonstrated the parameters of echocardiographic could be dependably determined in Shall sheep which, established normal reference values for these parameters and left ventricular function indices in healthy Shall sheep. These results can be beneficial in appropriate imagination, recognition and measuring cardiac structures.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study can be exerted as a reference for the assessment and diagnosis of heart diseases in sheep medicine and human cardiovascular research in sheep experimental models.

abstract.p: Graphical Abstract: Standing echocardiography was done from the right parasternal approach by B‐mode, M‐mode and Doppler systems to define the normal echocardiography values of heart in Iranian Shall male sheep. The results demonstrated that the echocardiographic parameters could be dependably determined in healthy Shall sheep, which established normal reference values for these parameters and left ventricular function indices. This study can exert as a reference for the diagnosis of heart diseases in sheep and human cardiovascular research in sheep experimental models.

p.mixed-citation: Sadeghian Chaleshtori, S., Mokber Dezfouli, M. R., Vajhi, A., Najd Ghahremani, A., Sabetzadeh, N., & Abbasi, J. (2024). Right parasternal echocardiographic reference values in the healthy male Iranian Shall sheep. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1535. 10.1002/vms3.153539049701

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Currently, echocardiography is an important noninvasive diagnostic method to evaluate the cardiovascular system in veterinary medicine. It has been widely employed in humans. Echocardiography is exerted for functional and structural assessment of the animal's heart (Amory & Kafidi, 1992; Crippa et al., 1992; Schiller et al., 1989). Because echocardiography is a noninvasive and harmless diagnostic tool, it is easy to use in sheep and goats (Hong et al., 2000; Mandour et al., 2020; Olsson et al., 2001).

Among the large animals, the heart of the sheep has a significant similarity with the human heart in terms of structural and functional parameters. The sheep heart, like the human heart, has a low ability to expand collateral blood flow after ischemic heart disease.

Sheep cardiac myocytes have close similarities to human cardiac myocytes because they have only 1–4 nuclei. The sheep is a suitable model for human chronic cardiac disease (Borenstein et al., 2006; Locatelli et al., 2011).

Recently, in research on the cardiovascular system and diseases of this system in humans, sheep are widely used as a laboratory model. This needs to know normal amounts of cardiac indices in different breeds of sheep. Moreover, due to slow growth rate, obedience, easy handling and long‐term follow‐up in cases of necessity, there is an emerging need for a documented echocardiography protocol for the sheep species (Hong et al., 2000; Veille et al., 1988).

Shall sheep originate from the Qazvin province of Iran. The characteristics of this breed include the following: The colour of the outer coat is often brown or black with white spots on the head front. The mean weight of ewes and rams is 61 and 82, respectively. Reproduction can occur at any time of the year but is usually in autumn and spring. One of the favourable features of this breed is its adaptability to different environmental conditions. Shall sheep have a higher food conversion coefficient with a more appropriate dressing ratio as well as a better mean twinning amount than other native sheep breeds in Iran. It is mostly preserved for meat production (Ghaffari et al., 2009; Ghavi Hossein‐Zadeh, 2015).

Due to the appropriate characteristics of sheep, attention to this animal as a model for cardiovascular research has increased, but alone a few studies have been done to distinguish the reference amount of echocardiography parameters in different breeds of sheep (Kumar et al., 2021). In addition, no study has been reported on Shall breed sheep that shows a complete set of heart dimensions and time indicators. Therefore, the purpose of this study was to report the quantitative echocardiographic measurements in Iranian Shall sheep from right side using various echocardiography modalities, including B‐mode, M‐mode, colour flow and pulsed wave Doppler.

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: To carry out this study, 20 Iranian Shall male sheep with weight of 30–35 kg and aged between 4 and 6 months which were clinically healthy were selected. To ensure the health of the sheep, in addition to auscultation to heart and pulmonary sounds, an electrocardiograph, complete blood count (CBC) and blood gases analysis were done. Moreover, the situation of heart valves was assessed with colour Doppler echocardiographic. The ration of the sheep was alfalfa, straw, silage and concentrate, and the water was available throughout the day and night. During the study, the sheep did not receive any sedatives or anaesthesia.

sec.title: Echocardiographic examination
sec.p: After the sheep calm down and adapt to the new environment and reduce environmental stress, examinations of echocardiography were done by a Micro Maxx Sonosite ultrasound system using a 2.5–5 MHz phased array transducer. All examinations of echocardiography were done in a standing position and without sedation in sheep, and all echocardiographic variables were measured in all sheep by one expert person (echoman). First, the wool on the right side of the chest was shaved from behind the elbow joint to the seventh rib. Then gel of ultrasound was put on the transducer, and echocardiography was performed from the third to fifth intercostal space on the right side in the views of longitudinal and transverse, and the left ventricle was assessed by systems of B‐mode, M‐mode and Doppler. Each parameter was obtained in three cardiac cycles, and their mean values were reported. The parameters of end‐systolic and end‐diastolic were recorded at the QRS complex onset of the electrocardiogram and the peak of the interventricular septum motion, respectively.

The parameters related to B‐mode (two‐dimensional) echocardiographic included the following: (1) views of the right parasternal long‐axis: view of the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) to evaluate the aortic valve, view of four chambers to evaluate the mitral valve and view of the right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) to evaluate the pulmonary and tricuspid valves and (2) views of right parasternal short‐axis to take pictures of M‐mode: view of the left ventricle at the chordae tendineae level, view of the mitral valve and view of aortic/left atrial appendage (LAA).

The parameters related to M‐mode echocardiographic included the following: (1) view of the right parasternal short‐axis at the chordae tendineae level to evaluate left ventricular free wall thickness at end‐systole and end‐diastole, interventricular septal thickness at end‐systole and end‐diastole, left ventricular internal diameter at end‐systole and end‐diastole, right ventricular internal diameter at end‐systole and end‐diastole, right ventricular free wall thickness at end‐systole and end‐diastole (Sadeghian Chaleshtori et al., 2023; Vloumidi & Fthenakis, 2017).

(2) Right parasternal short‐axis at the view of the mitral valve to assess end point septal separation of the mitral valve (the distance E point (the first peak of early diastolic filling) to interventricular septum) and mean velocity (V mean) of mitral valve closure in mid‐diastolic ejection fraction (EF slope).

(3) Right parasternal short‐axis at the view of the aortic valve to assess aortic root diameter at the end‐diastole, LAA in systole (the maximum internal diameter of the left atrium), the ratio of left atrial diameter to aortic valve diameter in diastole and left ventricular ejection time (from opening to closing of the aortic valve).

Left ventricular functional parameters included fractional shortening, end‐systolic volume, EF, stroke volume, end‐diastolic volume and cardiac output. They were computed using the following mentioned formulas (Kumar et al., 2021; Tavanaeimanesh et al., 2015): FS%=(LVIDd−LVIDs)/LVIDd×100 ESVmL=7×LVIDs3/2.4+LVIDs EDVmL=7×LVIDd3/2.4+LVIDd SVmL=EDV−ESV EF%=SV/EDV×100 COLit=SV×HR

For determination of the blood flow speed, it was used from right parasternal long‐axis at the LVOT view and by pulsed wave spectral Doppler echocardiographic was measured the parameters of the blood entering speed to the left ventricle through the mitral valve, including mitral valve V mean, mitral valve maximum velocity (V max), mitral valve mean pressure gradient (PGmean), mitral valve maximum pressure gradient (PGmax), mitral valve velocity time integral (VTI), mitral valve A‐wave (MV‐A), MV‐A pressure gradient, mitral valve E‐wave (MV‐E) and MV‐E pressure gradient as well as the parameters of the blood leaving speed from the left ventricle to the aorta, including aortic valve V mean, aortic V max, aortic valve PGmean, aortic valve PGmax and aortic valve VTI, and the other parameters, including LVOT‐V mean and LVOT‐V max, (Dezfouli et al., 2020).

One of the limitations that should be noted in the present study was the difficulty of evaluating Doppler echocardiography from the left apical view. Because in multi‐stomach animals and as has been previously reported in small ruminants, the presence of gas in the rumen and reticulum causes the echocardiography probe is not placed in the right direction and the obtained images are poor quality (Mandour, Elsayed et al., 2021; Mandour, Mahmoud et al., 2021). For this reason, it was not possible to prepare the quality images in this study; therefore, the evaluation of echocardiography was not performed on the left apical view.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The values were investigated with the Kolmogorov‐Smirnov test by the software of SPSS (version 24) for normal distribution. Descriptive statistics were stated as mean ± SD, range (minimum and maximum amounts) and 95% confidence interval. The association between the echocardiography parameters and weight was investigated via Pearson correlation test.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: All the sheep in this study were healthy and had no signs of heart disease. All the sheep were healthy in the clinical examination, and heart and lung sounds were normal. The heart rhythm was normal in the electrocardiography and no signs of arrhythmia were observed. There was no regurgitation in the assessment of heart valves by colour Doppler echocardiographic, and the health of valves was confirmed (Figure 1). Moreover, the blood gas analysis showed a high level of saturated oxygen and normal levels of PO2 and PCO2. In none of the sheep, an abnormal state was not observed in the CBC test.

caption.p: Colour flow Doppler echocardiographic views in Iranian Shall sheep. (a) Right parasternal long‐axis, the view of left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT). (b) Right parasternal short‐axis, the view of aortic valve. Ao, aorta; IVS, interventricular septum; LA, left atrium; LV, left ventricle; LVOT, left ventricle outflow tract; RA, right atrium; RV, right ventricle; RVOT, right ventricular outflow tract.

sec.p: Statistical analysis showed that the distribution of the echocardiographic measurements in Iranian Shall sheep was normal and the data were parametric. Pearson correlation test revealed the weight of the sheep had no relationship with the measured variables via the echocardiography.

Figure 2 demonstrates B‐mode echocardiographic views at end‐systole and end‐diastole. Images of right parasternal long‐axis views have been displayed so that Figure 2a–c shows views of four chambers, LVOT and RVOT, respectively, and Figure 2d shows a view of the right parasternal short‐axis.

caption.p: B‐mode echocardiographic views at end‐systole and end‐diastole in Iranian Shall sheep. (a) Right parasternal long‐axis, the view of four chambers. (b) Right parasternal long‐axis, the view of left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT). (c) Right parasternal long‐axis, the view of right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT). (d) Right parasternal short‐axis, view at the level of papillary muscles. Ao, aorta; AV, aortic valve; IVS, interventricular septum; LA, left atrium; LV, left ventricle; LVOT, left ventricle outflow tract; PA, pulmonary artery; RA, right atrium; RV, right ventricle; TV, tricuspid valve.

sec.p: Figure 3 displays M‐mode echocardiographic views at end‐systole and end‐diastole. Images of right parasternal short‐axis views have been revealed that Figure 3a–c shows views of the chordae tendineae, mitral valve and aortic valve, respectively. The value of measured parameters from these views is given as mean ± SD and range (minimum and maximum amounts) in Table 1.

caption.p: B‐ and M‐mode echocardiographic views at end‐systole and end‐diastole in Iranian Shall sheep. (a) The view of the right parasternal short‐axis, left ventricle at the chordae tendineae level + M‐mode at end‐diastole. (b) The view of the right parasternal short‐axis, left ventricle at the chordae tendineae level + M‐mode at end‐systole. (c) Right parasternal short‐axis, the view of mitral valve + M‐mode for assessment of mitral EPSS (end point septal separation of the mitral valve). There are two peaks E and A in each cardiac cycle. (e) The first larger peak demonstrates the mitral valve initial opening from passive circulation in early diastole, and (a) the second smaller peak demonstrates the atrial hurl that happens subsequently in diastole. (d) Right parasternal short‐axis, the view of aortic valve at the level of aorta and LA + M‐mode to measure LA/Ao ratio. Ao, aorta; LA, left atrium; MV, mitral valve.
The value of the measured parameters by M‐mode echocardiographic in Shall breed sheep and stated as mean ± SD and range (minimum and maximum amounts).

fn.p: Abbreviations: CI, confidence interval; CO, cardiac output; EF, ejection fraction; FS, fractional shortening; IVSd, interventricular septum diastole; IVSs, interventricular septum systole; LVFWd, left ventricular free wall diastole; LVFWs, left ventricular free wall systole; LVIDd, left ventricular internal diameters diastole; LVIDs, left ventricular internal diameters systole; RVFWd, right ventricular free wall diastole; RVFWs, right ventricular free wall systole; RVIDd, right ventricular internal diameter diastole; RVIDs, right ventricular internal diameter systole; SV, stroke volume.

sec.p: In addition, Figure 4 displays pulsed‐wave spectral Doppler echocardiographic in the right parasternal long‐axis at the LVOT view. So, Figure 4a shows the blood entering speed to the left ventricle through the mitral valve, and Figure 4b shows the blood leaving speed from the left ventricle to the aorta. The value of the measured parameters by spectral Doppler echocardiographic has stated as mean ± SD and range (minimum and maximum amounts) in Table 2.

caption.p: Pulsed wave spectral Doppler echocardiographic in right parasternal long‐axis at the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) view in Iranian Shall sheep. (a) Pulsed wave Doppler of diastolic mitral inflow showing upward early (e) and atrial (a) waves. (b) Doppler assessments of the aortic flow showing downward spectral flow.
The value of the measured parameters by pulsed wave spectral Doppler echocardiographic in Shall breed sheep and stated as mean ± SD and range (minimum and maximum amounts).

fn.p: Abbreviations: AoRD, aortic root diameter; AoV‐PGmax, aortic valve maximum pressure gradient; AoV‐PGmean, aortic valve mean pressure gradient; AoV‐V max, aortic valve maximum velocity; AoV‐V mean, aortic valve mean velocity; CI, confidence interval; EF, ejection fraction; EPSS, end point septal separation; LAA, left atrial appendage; LAD/AoVD, left atrial diameter/aortic valve diameter; LVET, left ventricular ejection time; LVOT‐V max, left ventricular outflow tract maximum velocity; LVOT‐V mean, left ventricular outflow tract mean velocity; MV‐A, mitral valve A‐wave; MV‐APG, mitral valve A‐wave pressure gradient; MV‐E, mitral valve E‐wave; MV‐EPG, mitral valve E‐wave pressure gradient; MV‐PGmax, mitral valve maximum pressure gradient; MV‐PGmean, mitral valve mean pressure gradient; MV‐V max, mitral valve maximum velocity; MV‐V mean, mitral valve mean velocity; MV‐VTI, mitral valve velocity time integral.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Echocardiography for evaluating functional and structural values of the cardiovascular system is the most sensitive and valuable method (Moses & Ross, 1987). Due to the placement of the heart inside the chest, echocardiography seems to be a suitable diagnostic technique. This is a procedure that, in large and small animals, is frequently used to assess changes in heart chamber size, wall thickness and valve function and form (Kobal & Petrič, 2007).

Currently, pigs and sheep are preferred as animal models for research on heart diseases. So, sheep are increasingly used in many research studies, on the one hand, because of slow growth rate, docility and easy handling, and on the other hand, because the sheep heart is more like the human heart in terms of structure and function compared to a pig. In human heart mimic studies, sheep are used for various evaluations, including assessing heart regeneration through the creation of different models, such as myocardial infarction or heart failure (Locatelli et al., 2011).

Although sheep are commonly used in cardiovascular research, due to the small acoustic views that are obtained through the cardiac notch, they are considered relatively hard species for evaluation of echocardiography. This is presumably because of the right accessory pulmonary lobe, which covers parts of the heart, especially the ventricles of the right and left along with the compaction of the air‐filled rumen vs. the diaphragm (Kirberger & Van Den Berg, 1993).

It has been reported that echocardiography measurement is related to breed, age, sex and body weight, and these can affect echocardiography measurement as confounding factors. In order to limit the influence of these confounding factors, in this study, we used male sheep of Iranian Shall breed in a certain age and weight range. According to the knowledge of the authors, in Shall sheep have not been reported the normal parameters of echocardiography and its left ventricular functional indices in the literature, and in general, little information has been reported on the echocardiography of sheep.

However, Aissi (2011) in vitro studied the anatomical structure of the normal heart via ultrasound of 27 healthy sheep hearts provided after killing and reported normal values. Locatelli et al. (2011), in healthy adult Corriedale sheep, obtained reference validity for echocardiography and indices of left ventricular function, and then the collected data compared with standardized values in humans.

Kumar et al. (2021) reference values of echocardiography in nonpregnant, healthy, adult, female Black Bengal goats and Muzaffarnagari sheep determined and compared them with each other. In the present study, the amounts of echocardiographic indices measured (mean ± SD) from the left heart in Iranian Shall sheep have been demonstrated in Tables 1 and 2, and the comparisons of these values with the studies of Kumar et al. (2021) and other researchers show that these values are standard.

Therefore, these amounts can be used as reference values in the echocardiography of Shall breed sheep. The data obtained in this research show that a simple and different approach can be used to achieve suitable echocardiography in Shall sheep. These normal values of echocardiography in healthy Shall sheep can be used as reference values when using this species as a laboratory animal and model studies on diseases of the human cardiovascular system as well as for the treatment and diagnosis of heart diseases in this breed of sheep.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: This study showed that a good quality echocardiogram can be prepared reproducibly in Iranian Shall sheep and provide the normal dimensions and functional parameters of the heart. The present study can be applied as a reference for more research on heart diseases in sheep, as well as in creating animal models to study the development and pathogenesis of heart diseases in humans.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Sirous Sadeghian Chaleshtori: The study design; performed the experiments; and the revision and finalization manuscript. Mohammad Reza Mokhber Dezfouli: The study design; commented on the data analysis and the revision and finalization of the manuscript. Alireza Vajhi: Performed; analysis and interpretation the data of echocardiography. Abolfazl Najd Ghahremani and Neda Sabetzadeh: Performed the experiments and prepared the initial manuscript. Javad Abbasi: Statistical analysis of data.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: All animal care and procedures were agreed upon by the Animal Ethics Committee, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran, and performed in accordance with international guidelines.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: This research was sponsored by the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF), vice‐presidency for Science, Technology, and Knowledge‐Based Economy, the project number 96006067. We are very grateful to the chairman and staff of the INSF, who sponsored this study.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: Authors want to share their data.
